## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Paces

### conntrack-provenance-negative-control (₢BVAAI) [rough]

## Character
Judgment plus a real fixture build; security-relevant. Closes epistemic debt the conntrack hardening incurred.

## Goal
conntrack_spoofed_ack now suppresses a would-be breach by L2 provenance, so its SECURE verdict is unfalsifiable — green could mean containment held, or that the probe/capture/classify pipeline silently broke (egress never left, AF_PACKET capture missed the reply, or the gateway-MAC compare is wrong). Build a deliberately-weakened-sentry nameplate whose loosened return path lets the lone-ACK reply transit the sentry (arriving with gateway-MAC provenance), and assert the sortie BREACHes there — proving the whole pipeline still goes red on a real return-path failure.

## Cinched
- Validates the SentryMediated -> BREACH direction only. OffPath -> SECURE suppression is certified solely by running on real Windows DD; the control does not prove quirk suppression.
- Pilots a discipline: any sortie that suppresses a would-be breach on an observation carries a negative control proving the suppression is conditional. This is currently the only such gate.
- Live upstream plus container runtime -> service/crucible tier, not fast. The new weakened-sentry nameplate is the real cost.

## Done
A weakened-sentry nameplate forwards the lone-ACK reply to the bottle; conntrack_spoofed_ack asserts BREACH against it and SECURE against the hardened nameplates, and the contrast runs green in the suite.

Sources: Memos/memo-20260604-conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication.md; RBSIP "Substrate Quirks" (rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply); sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack in rbida_sorties.rs; RBS0 Security Properties ("architectural reasoning alone has been empirically insufficient").

### offpath-blocked-dest-probe (₢BVAAJ) [rough]

## Character
Intricate but mostly mechanical — a near-variant of the existing provenance-classified sortie. Security-relevant.

## Goal
The rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply quirk is judged benign on one untested premise: the substrate answers only egress the sentry *allowed*, because blocked destinations are dropped at the sentry before the substrate ever sees them. Convert that to a tested fact — send the provenance-classified lone-ACK to a non-allowlisted destination; any reply, off-path or sentry-mediated, is a BREACH, because the substrate reached the bottle on behalf of a destination the sentry never approved (a real path-in, not a quirk).

## Cinched
- This is the empirical backstop for the quirk's keystone clause. A BREACH on Windows DD voids the quirk's benign classification and becomes a real finding.
- Unilateral; runs on the existing hardened nameplates, no new nameplate. The blocked-dest choice is a mount-time decision.

## Done
A provenance-aware lone-ACK to a blocked destination asserts no reply of any provenance; it runs SECURE across the platform matrix, or surfaces a real finding if it does not.

Sources: Memos/memo-20260604-conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication.md (Scope of exposure); RBSIP "Substrate Quirks" (rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply); sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack in rbida_sorties.rs.

### theurge-fast-suite-cygwin-bringup (₢BVAAB) [rough]

## Character
Integration gate — verification, plus whatever walls skirmish still surfaces.

## Goal
skirmish runs green over cygwin@rocket — the full standing-depot chain on an uncontrolled Cygwin host, not just the fast tier. The bar before the uncontrolled-Cygwin bringup is considered done.

## Landed (this heat — jjx_log BV)
- Fast tier green on cygwin@rocket (cat repair + the prior pace's launch/platform boundaries).
- theurge native config reads nativized on Cygwin (rbtdrx_path_from_env); canonical-invest proven green end-to-end on cygwin@rocket — governor mantle + retriever/director invest, real GCP IAM.
- rocket station BURS_TINCTURE aligned to the live canonical depot.

## Remaining
Re-run skirmish on cygwin@rocket; characterize and fix-or-scope each wall it surfaces (crucible-charge bind-mounts the next likely one). Payor OAuth on rocket expires periodically — refresh before a run.

## Done
skirmish green over cygwin@rocket.

### windows-docker-adapter-consolidation (₢BVAAG) [rough]

## Character
Design conversation, then a mechanical fold; needs judgment on the adapter's shape.

Three byte-identical /cygdrive path normalizers (zrbfc_native_path_capture, zrbndb_native_path_capture, zrbob_native_path_capture), the nested-mountpoint discipline (rbtid/project), and the rbgo headless-login auths-file bend are scattered Windows-docker membranes accreted across this heat. Fold them into one adapter. Off-Windows identity must be preserved (BURD_OSTYPE-gated); suites stay green. See Memos/memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md.

## Done
One canonical Windows-docker adapter; the duplicate normalizers removed; suites green on Cygwin and at least one non-Windows platform.

### cygwin-host-and-repo-winddown (₢BVAAH) [rough]

## Character
Cleanup; mostly mechanical, one decision (the kludge-stamp commits).

Wind down the spike's residue. On cygwin@rocket: remove probe scripts, the live-token ~/.docker/config.json, and GAR test artifacts (cygprobe/probe images, graft test hallmarks). Reconcile the cygwin-resident kludge-stamp commits (push as accepted temporary pollution, or discard). BCG-clean the rbgo login bend (the local declared mid-loop, the piped openssl capture). Regenerate the rbw-ts help string (still lists the pre-rename suite names) with its generated context doc.

## Done
cygwin@rocket and the repo clean; rbgo BCG debt cleared; rbw-ts help string current.

aiTitle: Identify local work tasks for tadmor testing

content.text: Let me read the paddock from the gazette.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BV
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Prove that Recipe Bottle's test harness (theurge) can run on an **uncontrolled**
6	Cygwin — a stock Windows+Cygwin box we do not provision. This is a consumer-
7	credibility claim: a consumer can qualify their own install on their own machine
8	and trust the verdict. It is deliberately distinct from the `rbk-NN-win-*` heats,
9	which are all controlled-infrastructure work (formal BURN garrison, remote
10	control, depot regime) on machines we own.
11	
12	## Progress & scope (updated 2026-06-03)
13	
14	The heat outgrew its original fast/validation-tier scope: the **crucible tier now
15	runs on uncontrolled Cygwin Docker Desktop**. siege (the renamed tadmor suite)
16	charges and runs the adversarial sortie set 45/46; blockade (moriah, airgap)
17	follows. The two membranes that made it possible — the compose-CLI path
18	normalizer and the nested bind-mountpoint pre-creation — are documented in
19	`Memos/memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`. This supersedes the
20	"container tiers run on WSL only / fast-tier only" locked decision below. One
21	open finding: the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie BREACHED on siege; adjudication
22	pending (`Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md`).
23	
24	## Remote build discipline (git is the filesystem)
25	
26	All work runs on a remote fundus (`cygwin@rocket`); code reaches it through git,
27	never scp. The cycle, learned the hard way this heat:
28	
29	1. Edit on the curia (this station).
30	2. Notch (`jjx_record`) — a real commit; the affiliation is the transport label.
31	3. `git pull --rebase` then `git push` on the curia.
32	4. On the fundus: `git pull` to fast-forward — or, when the fundus tree is dirty
33	   or detached (a prior `jjx_plant` or scratch state), `git fetch && git checkout
34	   -f main && git reset --hard origin/main`. Destructive git is sanctioned here
35	   only because it replaces with byte-identical committed content.
36	5. Run the tabtarget on the fundus over ssh. No trailing `echo` — it masks the
37	   exit code; let ssh propagate the tabtarget's status.
38	
39	Two traps that cost time this heat:
40	
41	- **scp is wrong.** It dirties the fundus tree and diverges from git. Git is the
42	  only sanctioned transport for code.
43	- **The fundus needs a git identity.** Without `git config user.name/email` on
44	  the fundus, any fixture that owns its own notch (kludge-tadmor and friends)
45	  dies with `Author identity unknown` (exit 128). Set it once per host.
46	
47	Exceptions and adjuncts:
48	
49	- **Kludge-mode crucibles cannot ride git-as-filesystem.** Kludge images are
50	  host-local — the image must be built on the fundus. So kludge on the fundus and
51	  let the fixture commit the stamp there; accept the temporary cygwin-resident
52	  stamp pollution. (Conjure-mode / blockade summons from GAR, so it rides git
53	  normally.)
54	- **Shared-depot credentials go stale.** Another host's governor mantle obsoletes
55	  this fundus's Director/Retriever SAs — they fail with `Invalid JWT Signature`
56	  (persisting past the retry budget, not a real propagation race). Re-establish
57	  before any GAR-touching run: reinstall the Payor OAuth, then run `dogfight`
58	  (its `canonical-invest` re-mantles the Governor and re-invests
59	  Retriever+Director).
60	
61	## Central thesis (the real problem)
62	
63	The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
64	(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
65	Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
66	bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
67	**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:
68	
69	- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
70	  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
71	  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
72	  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
73	- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
74	  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
75	  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
76	  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
77	  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.
78	
79	## Locked decisions
80	
81	- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
82	  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
83	  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
84	  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
85	  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
86	  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
87	- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
88	  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the

# jjezs_paddock ₣BV

## Shape

Prove that Recipe Bottle's test harness (theurge) can run on an **uncontrolled**
Cygwin — a stock Windows+Cygwin box we do not provision. This is a consumer-
credibility claim: a consumer can qualify their own install on their own machine
and trust the verdict. It is deliberately distinct from the `rbk-NN-win-*` heats,
which are all controlled-infrastructure work (formal BURN garrison, remote
control, depot regime) on machines we own.

## Progress & scope (updated 2026-06-03)

The heat outgrew its original fast/validation-tier scope: the **crucible tier now
runs on uncontrolled Cygwin Docker Desktop**. siege (the renamed tadmor suite)
charges and runs the adversarial sortie set 45/46; blockade (moriah, airgap)
follows. The two membranes that made it possible — the compose-CLI path
normalizer and the nested bind-mountpoint pre-creation — are documented in
`Memos/memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`. This supersedes the
"container tiers run on WSL only / fast-tier only" locked decision below. One
open finding: the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie BREACHED on siege; adjudication
pending (`Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md`).

## Remote build discipline (git is the filesystem)

All work runs on a remote fundus (`cygwin@rocket`); code reaches it through git,
never scp. The cycle, learned the hard way this heat:

1. Edit on the curia (this station).
2. Notch (`jjx_record`) — a real commit; the affiliation is the transport label.
3. `git pull --rebase` then `git push` on the curia.
4. On the fundus: `git pull` to fast-forward — or, when the fundus tree is dirty
   or detached (a prior `jjx_plant` or scratch state), `git fetch && git checkout
   -f main && git reset --hard origin/main`. Destructive git is sanctioned here
   only because it replaces with byte-identical committed content.
5. Run the tabtarget on the fundus over ssh. No trailing `echo` — it masks the
   exit code; let ssh propagate the tabtarget's status.

Two traps that cost time this heat:

- **scp is wrong.** It dirties the fundus tree and diverges from git. Git is the
  only sanctioned transport for code.
- **The fundus needs a git identity.** Without `git config user.name/email` on
  the fundus, any fixture that owns its own notch (kludge-tadmor and friends)
  dies with `Author identity unknown` (exit 128). Set it once per host.

Exceptions and adjuncts:

- **Kludge-mode crucibles cannot ride git-as-filesystem.** Kludge images are
  host-local — the image must be built on the fundus. So kludge on the fundus and
  let the fixture commit the stamp there; accept the temporary cygwin-resident
  stamp pollution. (Conjure-mode / blockade summons from GAR, so it rides git
  normally.)
- **Shared-depot credentials go stale.** Another host's governor mantle obsoletes
  this fundus's Director/Retriever SAs — they fail with `Invalid JWT Signature`
  (persisting past the retry budget, not a real propagation race). Re-establish
  before any GAR-touching run: reinstall the Payor OAuth, then run `dogfight`
  (its `canonical-invest` re-mantles the Governor and re-invests
  Retriever+Director).

## Central thesis (the real problem)

The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:

- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.

## Locked decisions

- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the
  "correct" answer — but it ships no precompiled std (needs nightly `-Z
  build-std`), and requires Cygwin `gcc` + LLVM ≥20.1. Fall back to it only if
  the native-binary path translation proves too leaky to finish Route A.
- **Toolchain installs are deferred to the experiment, not the dep pace.** The
  dep-install pace carries only route-independent knowns; `gcc`/`rust`/LLVM are
  Route-C-specific and must not be installed until/unless Route A is abandoned.
- **Scope is the runtime-free tier.** Container tiers (service/crucible) need a
  real container runtime; on Cygwin the only `docker` is Windows Docker Desktop,
  with a separate bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX does not cover.
  Per existing discipline, container tests run on the WSL side. This heat
  targets the **fast/validation** tier only.
  *(Superseded 2026-06-03 — see "Progress & scope" above: the crucible tier now
  runs on Cygwin Docker Desktop via the two Windows-docker membranes.)*

## What done looks like

`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` runs green over `cygwin@rocket`. A legitimate
alternative outcome is honest: the launch-boundary fix is proven and the
remaining work scoped, with heavier surgery deferred — the experiment is
exploratory and may reveal more than the release timeline wants to absorb.

## Known gaps to close

- **PATH visibility.** `cargo` exists on the host but is not on the
  non-interactive ssh command channel's PATH (rustup wired the Windows user
  PATH; the sshd-spawned shell that runs tabtargets does not see it). Make
  `.cargo/bin` resolvable to that shell — most robustly via the Windows *system*
  PATH or `~/.ssh/environment`.
- **The launch-boundary code change** in `rbtdri`/`rbtdrf` (above).
- **shellcheck path-argument boundary (deferred — off the fast-tier critical
  path).** `shellcheck` has no native Cygwin package (Haskell/GHC is
  incompatible with Cygwin's path layout); the install is the official Windows
  `shellcheck.exe` in `/usr/local/bin` (admin-placed — the unprivileged account
  cannot write there). It resolves and runs, but as a Windows-native tool it
  cannot open Cygwin `/`-style path arguments (`openBinaryFile: does not
  exist`) — it needs Windows-form paths (`cygpath -w`) or stdin. This is
  RBTDRX-family path translation but a **distinct site** from the `.sh`-launch
  boundary above: a Windows *tool* receiving Cygwin path *arguments*, not a
  `.sh` being `CreateProcess`'d. It surfaces only when the harness drives
  shellcheck in `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease — never in the fast/validation tier this
  heat targets. Close it if/when QualifyRelease is brought to Cygwin.

Starting point: officium open, BV groomed. Five paces remain on ₣BV. Here's how they sort against your two constraints — **₢BVAAG is live in the parallel chat** (hands off), and **no depot-affecting work** (no GAR, no governor/director/retriever invest, no Cloud Build, no shared-depot credential ops) — but local kludge + crucible/siege(tadmor) testing is fair game.

## Workable now

**₢BVAAJ — offpath-blocked-dest-probe** *(cleanest fit)*
A near-variant of the existing provenance-classified `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie. Its own docket cinches it: *"Unilateral; runs on the existing hardened nameplates, no new nameplate."* New sortie code in `rbida_sorties.rs`, kludge sentry+bottle locally, charge tadmor, run the case. No depot, no new nameplate — pure local security-sortie work. This is exactly the "locally kludge and test tadmor" lane.

**₢BVAAI — conntrack-provenance-negative-control** *(workable, one open question)*
The negative control: a deliberately-weakened-sentry nameplate that lets the lone-ACK reply transit, asserting BREACH there while the hardened nameplates stay SECURE. The sortie + sentry side is all local kludge. **Caveat I want to be honest about:** the docket cinches *"Live upstream plus container runtime → service/crucible tier… the new weakened-sentry nameplate is the real cost."* Whether that nameplate's *bottle* must be conjured from GAR (depot) or can ride a local kludge is a mount-time call I can't settle from the docket alone. If the bottle kludges, this is fully clear of the depot; if it wants a real conjure, it's blocked. Worth resolving first if you want to take it.

## Partially workable

**₢BVAAH — cygwin-host-and-repo-winddown**
Two of its sub-items are pure local curia work, no depot, no cygwin:
- BCG-clean the `rbgo` login bend (the local declared mid-loop, the piped openssl capture).
- Regenerate the `rbw-ts` help string (still lists pre-rename suite names) + its generated context doc.

The rest (GAR test-artifact removal, cygwin kludge-stamp reconcile) is depot/remote — deferred.

## Blocked now

- **₢BVAAB** — skirmish on cygwin@rocket: standing-depot chain + remote cygwin + OAuth refresh. Depot-affecting and parallel-chat-coupled.
- **₢BVAAG** — running in the parallel chat.

---

My recommendation: take **₢BVAAJ** — it's unambiguously inside your constraints and security-relevant. If you'd like a second front, I can confirm BVAAI's bottle-kludge question quickly, or knock out the two local slices of BVAAH. Which do you want to mount?

content.text:
I've traced the full chain. Here's the mount.

## Goal
Convert the off-path quirk's keystone premise — *"the substrate only ever answers egress the sentry allowed, because blocked destinations are dropped before the substrate sees them"* — into a tested fact: send the same provenance-classified lone-ACK as `conntrack_spoofed_ack`, but to a **blocked** destination, and assert that **no reply of any provenance** comes back. A reply (off-path *or* sentry-mediated) voids the quirk's benign classification.

## What I found
The existing `conntrack_spoofed_ack` already gives me everything reusable:
- `send_lone_ack_classify_provenance()` — sends the lone ACK, captures at L2, returns `AckProvenance::{NoResponse, SentryMediated, OffPath, Indeterminate}`.
- The only thing that changes for this variant is **the destination** (blocked, not the resolved allowed domain) and **the verdict mapping**.

The variant's judgment inverts the off-path arm:

| Provenance | `conntrack_spoofed_ack` (allowed dst) | **this sortie (blocked dst)** |
|---|---|---|
| NoResponse | SECURE | **SECURE** (sentry dropped egress — premise holds) |
| OffPath | SECURE (benign quirk) | **BREACH** (substrate answered for a dest the sentry never approved — voids the quirk) |
| SentryMediated | BREACH | **BREACH** (egress allowlist broken) |
| Indeterminate | BREACH | **BREACH** (conservative) |
| socket Err | SECURE | **SECURE** |

## Two mount-time decisions (resolving them; flag if you disagree)
- **Selector/mint:** `offpath-blocked-dest` — enum `OffpathBlockedDest`, fn `sortie_offpath_blocked_dest`, fixture wrapper `rbtdrc_sortie_offpath_blocked_dest`. I deliberately did *not* reuse the `conntrack-*` family — the keystone here is the egress allowlist + the off-path premise, not conntrack state, so a `conntrack-` name would mislead. Matches the silks.
- **Blocked destination:** `8.8.8.8`. It's the suite-wide convention for a forbidden dest (used in `net_forbidden_cidr`, `udp_non_dns_blocked`, `net_srcip_spoof`, etc.) — outside every allowed CIDR on any platform.

## Touch points (mechanical, mirrors the sibling exactly)
- `rbida_attacks.rs` — 5 sites: SEL const, enum variant, `from_str` arm, selector arm, dispatch arm.
- `rbida_sorties.rs` — new `sortie_offpath_blocked_dest()` (reuses the provenance helper).
- `rbtdrc_crucible.rs` — wrapper + one `case!()` in `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` (shared by siege/tadmor and blockade/moriah).

## Local expectation
On a healthy non-Windows-DD substrate the blocked-dest egress is dropped at the sentry → `NoResponse` → **SECURE**. So locally this proves the sortie wires up and goes green; the actual quirk-falsification finding can only emerge on Windows DD (out of scope here, runs there later).

## Strategy
Sequential, single agent (me), opus — three files, tightly coupled, security-relevant judgment. Build `rbw-tb`, kludge the bottle (carries the ifrit binary), charge tadmor, run the single case.

Proceed?

# jjezs_reslate BVAAJ

## Character
Intricate but mostly mechanical — a near-variant of the existing provenance-classified sortie. Security-relevant.

## Goal
The rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply quirk is judged benign on one untested premise: the substrate answers only egress the sentry *allowed*, because blocked destinations are dropped at the sentry before the substrate ever sees them. Convert that to a tested fact — send the provenance-classified lone-ACK to a non-allowlisted destination; any reply, off-path or sentry-mediated, is a BREACH, because the substrate reached the bottle on behalf of a destination the sentry never approved (a real path-in, not a quirk).

## Cinched
- This is the empirical backstop for the quirk's keystone clause. A BREACH on Windows DD voids the quirk's benign classification and becomes a real finding.
- Unilateral; runs on the existing hardened nameplates, no new nameplate. The blocked-dest choice is a mount-time decision.
- Selector `offpath-blocked-dest` (deliberately NOT the conntrack-* family — the keystone is the egress allowlist + off-path premise, not conntrack state); blocked dest is 8.8.8.8 (suite-wide forbidden-dest convention).

## Landed
- Sortie written and wired (commit e730fee5): ifrit selector/enum/from_str/as_str/dispatch + sortie_offpath_blocked_dest reusing send_lone_ack_classify_provenance; theurge wrapper + RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY case (runs in siege/tadmor and blockade/moriah).
- **Passes in current form on macOS** — siege green 61/61, NoResponse -> SECURE (sentry dropped egress to 8.8.8.8). This proves the control compiles on Linux, wires end-to-end, and goes green on a healthy substrate.

## Remaining (the load-bearing legs)
The control's teeth are on Windows Docker Desktop, where the off-path quirk actually lives. Must run green (or surface a real finding) on **both the WSL and the Cygwin users of rocket** — not just one. macOS green alone does not close this pace.

## Done
offpath-blocked-dest runs SECURE (or surfaces a real finding) on the WSL and Cygwin rocket substrates, in addition to the confirmed macOS pass.

Sources: Memos/memo-20260604-conntrack-spoofed-ack-adjudication.md (Scope of exposure); RBSIP "Substrate Quirks" (rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply); sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack in rbida_sorties.rs.

Reslated ₢BVAAJ — macOS pass recorded, WSL + Cygwin rocket legs now stated as the load-bearing remainder.

## The other test-adding pace: ₢BVAAI — conntrack-provenance-negative-control

The companion negative control on the *same* sortie family — and it's the more involved one:

**Goal:** `conntrack_spoofed_ack` now *suppresses* a would-be breach via L2 provenance (OffPath → SECURE), which makes its SECURE verdict unfalsifiable — green could mean containment held, *or* that the probe/capture/classify pipeline silently broke (egress never left, AF_PACKET capture missed the reply, gateway-MAC compare is wrong). This pace builds a **deliberately-weakened-sentry nameplate** whose loosened return path lets the lone-ACK reply transit the sentry (arriving with gateway-MAC provenance), and asserts the sortie **BREACHes** there — proving the whole pipeline still goes red on a real return-path failure.

**Cinched:**
- Validates the SentryMediated → BREACH direction only (the OffPath → SECURE suppression is certified separately, on real Windows DD).
- Pilots a discipline: any sortie that suppresses a would-be breach carries a negative control proving the suppression is conditional. Currently the only such gate.
- The new **weakened-sentry nameplate is the real cost** — service/crucible tier, not fast.

**Where it differs from what we just did:** ₢BVAAJ was unilateral (existing hardened nameplates, no new infra). ₢BVAAI needs a *new* weakened-sentry nameplate built — that's the open question I flagged at groom: whether its bottle can ride a local kludge or wants a real conjure (depot). Worth settling that first thing when we mount it.

Ready to mount ₢BVAAI when you are.

40	ip -o -4 addr show scope global > "${z_temp_file}" || exit 11
41	RBJ_UPLINK_IF=""
42	while read z_num z_ifname z_rest; do
43	  test "${z_ifname}" = "${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}" && continue
44	  RBJ_UPLINK_IF="${z_ifname}"
45	  break
46	done < "${z_temp_file}"
47	test -n "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" || { echo "FATAL: No uplink interface found"; exit 11; }
48	
49	rm -f "${z_temp_file}"
50	echo "RBJp1: Enclave interface = ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}"
51	echo "RBJp1: Uplink interface  = ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"
52	
53	# Docker assigns the container's default route to whichever attached network's
54	# gateway it picks (alphabetically-first attached, empirically); compose 'priority'
55	# is not honored. Same don't-trust-Docker discipline as interface naming above —
56	# sentry takes ownership: derive uplink gateway as first usable IP of uplink
57	# subnet (Docker bridge convention) and install default route via uplink.
58	echo "RBJp1: Computing uplink gateway from connected subnet"
59	z_uplink_addr_cidr=$(ip -o -4 addr show dev "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" | awk '{print $4; exit}')
60	test -n "${z_uplink_addr_cidr}" || { echo "FATAL: No IPv4 address on uplink interface ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"; exit 12; }
61	z_uplink_ip="${z_uplink_addr_cidr%/*}"
62	z_uplink_prefix="${z_uplink_addr_cidr#*/}"
63	
64	IFS=. read z_o1 z_o2 z_o3 z_o4 <<EOF_OCTETS
65	${z_uplink_ip}
66	EOF_OCTETS
67	
68	z_host_bits=$(( 32 - z_uplink_prefix ))
69	z_ip_int=$(( (z_o1 << 24) | (z_o2 << 16) | (z_o3 << 8) | z_o4 ))
70	z_mask=$(( 0xFFFFFFFF ^ ((1 << z_host_bits) - 1) ))
71	z_net_int=$(( z_ip_int & z_mask ))
72	z_gw_int=$(( z_net_int + 1 ))
73	RBJ_UPLINK_GW="$(( (z_gw_int >> 24) & 0xFF )).$(( (z_gw_int >> 16) & 0xFF )).$(( (z_gw_int >> 8) & 0xFF )).$(( z_gw_int & 0xFF ))"
74	echo "RBJp1: Uplink gateway     = ${RBJ_UPLINK_GW}"
75	
76	echo "RBJp1: Replacing default route via uplink (sentry-side ownership)"
77	ip route replace default via "${RBJ_UPLINK_GW}" dev "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" || exit 13
78	ip -o -4 route show default
79	
80	echo "RBJp1: Beginning IPTables initialization"
81	
82	echo "RBJp1: Set ephemeral port range for uplink connections"
83	echo "${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN} 65535" > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range || exit 10
84	
85	echo "RBJp1: Flushing existing rules"
86	iptables -F        || exit 10
87	iptables -t nat -F || exit 10
88	
89	echo "RBJp1: Setting default policies"
90	iptables -P INPUT   DROP || exit 10
91	iptables -P FORWARD DROP || exit 10
92	iptables -P OUTPUT  DROP || exit 10
93

# sentry takes ownership: derive uplink gateway as first usable IP of uplink
# subnet (Docker bridge convention) and install default route via uplink.
echo "RBJp1: Computing uplink gateway from connected subnet"

test -n "${z_uplink_addr_cidr}" || { echo "FATAL: No IPv4 address on uplink interface ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"; exit 12; }

${z_uplink_ip}

z_ip_int=$(( (z_o1 << 24) | (z_o2 << 16) | (z_o3 << 8) | z_o4 ))

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# srjcl post-charge hook
#
# Emits the JupyterLab URL and warns if the operator's shell did not export
# ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (in which case notebook cells that call the Anthropic API
# will fail authentication).
#
# Self-contained by contract: no BUK/RBK utility-module dependencies. Inherits
# RBRN_*/RBRR_* environment from the charge process.

set -eu

readonly z_yellow=$'\033[33m'
readonly z_bold=$'\033[1m'
readonly z_reset=$'\033[0m'

readonly z_url="http://localhost:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}/lab"
printf '%sJupyterLab ready:%s %s\n' "${z_bold}" "${z_reset}" "${z_url}"

if test -z "${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY:-}"; then
  printf '%sWARNING:%s ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set in your shell — Anthropic API calls in the notebook will fail.\n' "${z_yellow}" "${z_reset}"
  printf '  Remediation: %sexport ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-...%s in your shell, then re-charge srjcl to pick it up.\n' "${z_bold}" "${z_reset}"
fi

content.text:
I now have the full shape. Here's the mount.

## Goal
Make `conntrack_spoofed_ack`'s SECURE verdict falsifiable. Today its OffPath→SECURE *and* NoResponse→SECURE arms mean green could equally mean "containment held" or "the probe/capture/classify pipeline silently broke." This pace builds a deliberately-weakened-sentry nameplate whose loosened return path lets the lone-ACK reply transit the sentry (gateway-MAC provenance → SentryMediated), and asserts the sortie **BREACHes** there — proving the pipeline still goes red on a real return-path failure.

## What I found
- **No depot needed — confirmed.** tadmor's sentry hallmark is `k260605085702-e730fee5d` — a kludge stamp from my commit. Both sentry and bottle vessels ride local kludge.
- **The verdict inverts.** `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit` maps ifrit PASS→fixture Pass, BREACH→Fail. The negative control needs the opposite: the fixture must **pass when ifrit reports a SentryMediated BREACH** — and *only* that, not any failure (a pipeline error is also a Fail; counting it would re-defeat falsifiability). So the inverted wrapper must assert specifically on the `SentryMediated` arm.
- **The weakening lever (hypothesis, to confirm empirically first):** the sentry has no explicit `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=0` (rbjs_sentry.sh:99-100 is the only return-path rule). The lone-ACK reply transits only if conntrack tracks the out-of-state flow loosely, giving the masquerade a reverse-NAT mapping so the reply returns ESTABLISHED via the gateway. **First build action:** charge tadmor, flip the tunable on the sentry via `writ`, re-run `conntrack-spoofed-ack`, watch it go SentryMediated/BREACH. If the lever turns out elsewhere, the experiment redirects the design before any nameplate is built.
- **Pieces to build:** a weakened twin nameplate of tadmor (same kludged vessels + a weakening mechanism), a new crucible fixture (charge → run sortie → assert BREACH inverted), service/crucible tier.

## The one genuine fork
Where the weakening lives is an architectural call with real trade-offs — and it changes the build materially. Let me get your read before I commit.

content.text:
Yes — that's not only reasonable, it's the better design, and there's direct precedent for it.

## Precedent: the `rbtdrc_coordinated_*` family
The entire coordinated-case family is **theurge owning the dynamics** — it `writ`s into the sentry and `bark`s into the bottle around a single judged interaction. The closest shape is `rbtdrc_coordinated_dns_cache_integrity` (crucible.rs:1493):

> discover sentry IP → **writ** pre-snapshot → **bark** an ifrit attack → **writ** post-snapshot → judge the composite.

`rbtdrc_writ` runs arbitrary commands in the sentry (it's how the egress-lockdown and dns-cache cases work), so theurge can mutate the sentry's return path directly — `iptables`/`sysctl` — then bark the sortie, then judge. No precedent gap to invent.

## What that buys us — and it reframes a cinch
If theurge owns it, the negative control becomes **one coordinated case on the existing tadmor crucible**, not a new nameplate:

1. weaken the sentry return path via `writ` (the lever — confirm empirically first);
2. `bark conntrack-spoofed-ack` → assert **BREACH + SentryMediated** (gateway-MAC provenance — the reply genuinely transits the sentry, so it's a true L2-gateway source, exactly what the sortie's breach arm wants);
3. **restore** via `writ` (mandatory — the security fixture runs all cases on one charge; a left-weakened sentry would poison later cases).

This is strictly better on the containment axis than the hook: the weakening is **ephemeral** — in-memory in the sentry's iptables/sysctl for the duration of one case, never persisted in any nameplate or image. The control travels *with* the sortie's own fixture instead of as separate infra — which is exactly the discipline the docket wants to pilot.

**The tradeoff to name honestly:** the hook/nameplate approach leaves a reusable artifact an operator could charge and poke by hand; the theurge-owned approach is test-internal only — not manually reproducible without the harness. For a negative control whose sole consumer is the suite, I think that's the right call, but it's your call to make.

**It also re-opens a cinch.** The docket cinched *"the new weakened-sentry nameplate is the real cost."* Going theurge-owned drops that cost entirely — no new nameplate. If you're good with the direction, I'll update the docket to reflect it.

## Still load-bearing regardless of site
- **The lever** (confirm empirically before building): I believe it's `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` — with loose tracking the out-of-state ACK gets conntrack state, the masquerade gets a reverse mapping, and the reply returns ESTABLISHED via the gateway. First action: charge tadmor, `writ` the tunable on the sentry, re-run the sortie, watch it flip to SentryMediated. If the lever's elsewhere, the experiment redirects us before any code.
- **The inverted assertion must be specific** — pass only on `SentryMediated`, not on any Fail (a pipeline error is also a Fail; counting it would re-defeat the falsifiability this whole pace exists to establish).

Want me to proceed on the theurge-owned coordinated-case design — starting with the empirical lever confirmation against a live tadmor crucible?

message.content: Restate in clearer simpler language.  Clearly identify any concerns.

content.text:
## The idea, plainly

Instead of building a whole new "weakened sentry" nameplate, have **theurge itself** weaken the sentry on the fly during one test, then put it back. theurge already does this kind of thing — several existing tests reach into the sentry, run a command, poke the bottle, and judge what happens. So this isn't a new trick.

The test would do four steps on the normal tadmor crucible:

1. **Weaken** the sentry's return path (one command into the sentry).
2. **Run** the `conntrack-spoofed-ack` sortie.
3. **Check** it now reports a real breach (the reply came back through the sentry).
4. **Restore** the sentry to normal.

That's the whole test. No new nameplate, no new image, no change to the real sentry's code. The weakening exists only in memory, only for those few seconds.

## Why I prefer this over the earlier "hook on a new nameplate" idea

- It's cheaper — nothing new to build and maintain.
- The real sentry code stays untouched.
- The weakening can never leak into production, because it's never written down anywhere.

## My concerns (the honest list)

1. **It changes a decision you'd already locked.** The pace's notes say the new nameplate "is the real cost" — i.e., you'd planned to build one. This approach throws that away. That's fine if you agree, but I want you to say so, not assume it.

2. **Restore must be reliable.** All the security tests share one charged crucible. If step 4 fails to put the sentry back, every test after this one runs against a weakened sentry and the results are garbage. The restore has to be bulletproof (and ideally verified before moving on).

3. **I don't yet know the exact weakening lever.** My best guess is one kernel setting (`nf_conntrack_tcp_loose`). I haven't proven it. So the first thing I'd do is a throwaway experiment on a live tadmor crucible — flip the setting, run the sortie, see if it actually flips to a breach. If my guess is wrong, we rethink before writing any test code.

4. **The "breach" check must be precise.** The test should pass *only* when the breach is the real thing (reply came through the sentry gateway). If I let it pass on *any* failure, a broken pipeline would look like success — which is the exact problem this test exists to catch.

5. **You lose a hand-pokeable artifact.** With a separate weakened nameplate, you could charge it and experiment by hand. With this approach, the weakening only happens inside the test run. For a test-only safety check, I think that's fine — but it's a real difference.

Want me to start with concern #3 — the throwaway experiment to confirm the lever — before committing to anything?

content.text:
Checked the engine. The key fact: **charge and quench already happen per *fixture*, not per case.** Every fixture is `{ setup: charge, teardown: quench, cases: [...] }`. The security fixture charges once, runs all ~60 cases, quenches once. That's the current design.

So there are really three ways to get isolation, and the engine hands us the cheapest one for free.

## The three options, simply

**1. Charge/quench between every case (your first idea)**
- Clean: every case starts fresh.
- But: ~60 charge cycles per run, times two fixtures. Each charge spins up containers + sets up firewall + waits for readiness. This turns a couple of minutes into many. The shared crucible exists *specifically* to avoid this cost.
- Also needs an engine change — setup/teardown is fixture-level today, not case-level.

**2. Charge/quench only around the cases that dirty the sentry (your second idea)**
- Cheap, and isolates exactly where needed.
- But: the engine has to *learn* which cases are "dirty" — a new flag on each case plus new logic to re-charge after one. That's real new machinery, built for a single case today.

**3. Put the weakening test in its own fixture (what I'd recommend)**
- A fixture *is* the charge/quench boundary, and it already exists. So: a dedicated fixture that charges tadmor, weakens → runs sortie → checks for breach, then quenches.
- The quench at the end **throws the whole crucible away** — so there's nothing to restore, and a forgotten restore *can't* poison anything. The fragile step from my earlier concern list simply disappears.
- Cost: one extra charge cycle. No engine change at all.
- Bonus: you can run it alone with `rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh` for debugging.

## My recommendation
Option 3. It gives you the isolation you're rightly asking for, costs one charge cycle instead of sixty, needs zero new engine code, and **kills the restore-reliability concern outright** — which was the real worry.

## The one rule that comes with it
Within a single fixture, cases still share that fixture's crucible. So the weakening case must be **the only mutating case in its fixture** (or do its own weaken→test→assert all inside one case, like the dns-cache case does pre/post in one go). Keep the negative-control fixture to that one self-contained case and the rule takes care of itself.

This also fits the docket's "pilots a discipline" note: as more negative controls appear, each one either joins this isolated fixture or gets its own — both lean on the boundary that already exists, no new mechanism ever.

Want to go with the dedicated-fixture approach? If so, my first step is still the throwaway experiment to confirm the weakening lever before writing anything.

content.text:
Key finding — my hypothesis is **wrong**: `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose=1` already (kernel default), yet the baseline is SECURE/NoResponse. So the hardened sentry isn't relying on that tunable. The silence likely comes from the *remote* simply not replying to an out-of-state ACK — which is exactly why this verdict is "unfalsifiable."

That reframes the lever: I can't rely on weakening conntrack, and I shouldn't depend on a remote host choosing to reply (non-deterministic). The deterministic, self-contained way to produce a gateway-sourced reply is to make the **sentry itself** answer — insert a `REJECT --reject-with tcp-reset` so the sentry manufactures an RST sourced from the destination, delivered back via the gateway. Let me test that.

Decisive: even with OUTPUT allowing RST, the capture shows **only the outbound ACK** — `REJECT --reject-with tcp-reset` emits no reply at all here. REJECT is dead as a lever. I've cleaned up the experiment rules (REJECT + OUTPUT-allow removed). Stopping to report before grinding further — this hit a real wall and the path forward is a scope decision that's yours.

## What the experiment proved

1. **My guessed lever was wrong.** `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` is already `1` (loose) on the hardened sentry. It's not what holds the line.

2. **The hardened SECURE rests on the *remote host's silence*, not sentry enforcement.** The capture shows the lone ACK leaves the enclave (bottle→internic) and **nothing comes back** — internic just doesn't answer an out-of-state ACK. So today's "SECURE: return-path state enforcement intact" is really "no reply observed," which partly depends on how a remote internet host behaves. *That is itself the unfalsifiability this pace exists to expose* — and it's worth noting the existing verdict slightly overclaims.

3. **To force a breach, a TCP frame must reach the bottle from the sentry's MAC** (that's what `SentryMediated` means). Only the sentry can emit such a frame.

4. **No single firewall rule makes the sentry emit one.** REJECT fires (counter increments) but produces no packet toward the bottle, with or without an OUTPUT allowance — proven by capture, twice.

5. **Tooling is thin:** the bottle has no python/tcpdump; the sentry has tcpdump but no packet crafter (no python3/hping/nping).

## The fork (this is the real decision)

The elegant "theurge weakens the sentry with one `writ`" picture doesn't survive contact — there's no one-rule weakening that yields a gateway-sourced reply. The deterministic options are:

- **(A) Redirect to a real responder via DNAT.** Add sentry rules that DNAT the bottle's `:80` egress to an enclave host (e.g. the pentacle). Any TCP stack RSTs a lone ACK, conntrack reverse-NATs the RST so it appears to come from internic, and it returns via the sentry → `SentryMediated` → BREACH. Stays theurge-owned, iptables-only, no vessel change — **but it's a small rule-set, not one line, and leans on an enclave host answering.**

- **(B) Give the sentry a packet injector.** Add a crafting tool (python3/scapy or a tiny static binary) to the sentry vessel so it injects a spoofed reply directly. Most faithful and deterministic — **but it reopens the "new tooling / vessel change" scope we were specifically trying to avoid.**

## My read (sincere)

I lean **(A)** — it keeps the theurge-owned, no-new-artifact shape we agreed on. But it's more effort than the plan assumed, and there's a deeper question worth your eyes: since the natural SECURE rests on remote silence, this control can only ever *synthesize* a breach, never reproduce a real one on Linux. That's still valuable (it proves the capture/classify pipeline goes red), but it's worth deciding whether that's worth the rule-set, or whether the better investment is hardening the sortie itself to not depend on remote behavior.

The crucible is **still charged** if you want me to test (A) right now. How do you want to proceed?

content.text:
This proceed uncovered the thing that actually reshapes the pace — so let me stop and put it in front of you rather than invent increasingly fragile hacks.

## The architecture finding that changes everything

The enclave has **no third host to use as a responder.** Ground truth from docker:

- **enclave (10.242.0.0/24):** sentry `.2`, and `.3` = **pentacle**
- **transit (172.18.0.0/16):** sentry only
- **bottle:** no network of its own

The bottle and pentacle **share one network namespace** (`.3`) — the pentacle is a netns sidecar, not a separate machine. So the only host the bottle can talk to on the enclave is the sentry. There is nothing on the enclave or transit that will answer a lone ACK and send the reply back through the sentry.

That guts option (A): **"DNAT to a real responder" has no responder.** Internic stays silent (proven), the sentry won't reply (INPUT DROP), and there's no third party.

## Why the simple levers all failed (the honest tally)

- `nf_conntrack_tcp_loose` — already loose; not the lock.
- The hardened SECURE = **the remote simply doesn't answer** (captured: ACK leaves, nothing returns). Not sentry enforcement.
- `REJECT --reject-with tcp-reset` — fires (counter ticks) but **emits no packet** toward the bottle, with or without an OUTPUT allowance (captured twice). Dead.
- No responder exists to DNAT toward.

The common root: **on Linux, nothing naturally sends a gateway-sourced reply to the bottle.** That packet only ever exists if the sentry *fabricates and injects* it — and the sentry has tcpdump but no packet crafter (no python/hping/nping).

## The real fork (yours to call)

1. **Give the sentry an injector (option B).** Sentry crafts the reply (src=dst:80 → bottle, RST), injects it on the enclave → gateway MAC → `SentryMediated` → BREACH. Deterministic and faithful — **but** needs a tool the sentry lacks (vessel change), *and* needs theurge to fire the injection concurrently with the sortie's 3s listen (bark is synchronous) — real orchestration, no longer "one writ."
2. **A multi-rule NAT reflection** — make the sentry bounce the bottle's own ACK back, rewritten to look like a reply from the dst. Pure iptables, auto-firing — **but** relies on a conntrack double-rewrite (src and dst both transit through the bottle's address) that I'm not confident is robust, and it's IP/port-templated. I'd be guessing on reliability.
3. **Reconsider the control's design** — it's materially harder than the docket assumed; the "theurge weakens with a writ" picture doesn't survive the architecture.

## My sincere read

The cheap, elegant version we cinched doesn't exist. The only *deterministic, faithful* path is (B), and it costs a sentry-side tool plus concurrency — which reopens exactly the scope we tried to avoid. I don't think I should pick that for you silently.

**Separately — a real finding worth keeping regardless:** the existing `conntrack_spoofed_ack` SECURE verdict rests partly on the *remote host's silence*, not purely on sentry enforcement. Its "return-path state enforcement intact" message overclaims. That likely deserves its own note or itch no matter how this pace resolves.

The crucible is **still charged and back to stock** (experiment rules removed). How do you want to steer — commit to (B) with the added cost, let me actually test (2) to see if it holds, or pause and reslate the pace with these findings?

content.text:
Cleaned up — sentry's back to stock. Here's the plain version.

## What I hit (in one sentence)
The spoofed ACK is "invalid" as far as the firewall's connection-tracker is concerned, and **the firewall's address-rewriting machinery skips invalid packets entirely** — so no firewall rule can turn that packet into a fake reply. I proved it: my rewrite rule sat at **zero matches**. The earlier RST trick emitted nothing either. The firewall simply has no lever here.

## What that means
The only way to make a reply appear from the sentry is for the sentry to **build a brand-new packet from scratch and inject it**. The sentry has no tool that can do that (no Python, no packet crafter — just tcpdump, which only watches). So the cheap "theurge pokes the sentry with one command" idea genuinely cannot work. I'm confident now, not guessing.

## Your three real choices

**1. Cheap and still load-bearing — a self-check (my recommendation).**
Have the test feed the bottle's own detector a known fake reply and confirm it correctly yells "BREACH." This proves the detection code (capture + the MAC comparison) isn't silently dead — which is exactly the rot the pace worried about. No sentry change, no new tools. It won't exercise the firewall end-to-end, but it closes most of the gap for little cost.

**2. Faithful but expensive — give the sentry a packet-crafting tool.**
Add a small injector to the sentry image and have theurge fire it mid-test. This is the "real" end-to-end version. But it's a vessel rebuild plus timing coordination, and on Linux the breach is *synthetic either way* — so you pay a lot for fidelity that's still artificial.

**3. Do almost nothing — just fix the lie.**
The current pass message says "return-path enforcement intact." That overclaims: I proved the pass really just means "the remote host stayed quiet." Correct the wording and write down the limitation.

## My advice
Do **#1 plus #3**: build the self-check (real value, low cost) and fix the misleading message (a genuine accuracy bug I uncovered). Treat #2 as a separate, larger decision for later if you ever want true end-to-end. 

Want me to go ahead with #1 + #3?

Both confirmed working in the live run. Plain summary:

## What's done (and verified)
- **The self-check (#1)** — a new test that feeds the detector five known fake frames and checks it labels each correctly (gateway reply → breach, off-path → suppressed, etc.). If anyone ever breaks the packet-parsing offsets or flips the MAC comparison, **this test goes red** — that's the load-bearing guarantee the pace wanted: a SECURE verdict can no longer hide a dead detector. To get there I also pulled the parse+classify logic into one shared function that both the live probe and the self-check use.
- **The honesty fix (#3)** — the old "return-path enforcement intact" message is gone; it now plainly says it only observed silence (which a quiet remote also causes) and points at the self-check for liveness.
- **siege is green, 62/62** on macOS, including the new case. Committed as `770a2aec`.

## Two things for your call

1. **The kludge re-stamped `tadmor/rbrn.env`** with fresh local hallmarks (uncommitted). That's the same kludge-stamp residue your heat already tracks under the wind-down pace — I left it uncommitted rather than decide for you (commit as a transport label, or discard).

2. **The ₢BVAAI docket still describes the old plan** (build a weakened-sentry nameplate). What we actually built is the self-check reframe, after proving the weakened-sentry path can't work cheaply on Linux. I'd like to reslate the docket to match reality before we lose the reasoning.

## Same caveat as before
This passed on **macOS only**. Like ₢BVAAJ, the WSL + Cygwin rocket legs aren't exercised here.

I'm **not wrapping** — your call. Want me to reslate ₢BVAAI to capture what we built and learned, and how do you want to handle the rbrn.env stamp?

content.text:
Yes — I recommend it, and for ₢BVAAJ specifically it's not optional polish: the docket makes **WSL + Cygwin on rocket the load-bearing legs**, because the offpath-blocked-dest sortie's whole reason to exist is the Windows Docker Desktop substrate where the off-path quirk actually lives. macOS green only proves it wires up. (Bonus: the same siege run also re-verifies ₢BVAAI's pipeline self-check on those platforms.)

Here's a self-contained prompt for a clean chat:

---

```
Mount ₣BV and run the cross-platform verification leg for pace ₢BVAAJ
(offpath-blocked-dest-probe) on the rocket test host.

BACKGROUND (already landed on main, verified on macOS only):
- A new ifrit sortie `offpath-blocked-dest` (selector), theurge case
  `rbtdrc_sortie_offpath_blocked_dest` — sends a provenance-classified lone
  ACK to a blocked destination (8.8.8.8); any reply of any provenance is a
  BREACH, only silence is SECURE. Commit e730fee5.
- A new self-check case `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_pipeline_selfcheck` (pace
  ₢BVAAI, already wrapped) — pure parse+classify logic check, must pass on
  every platform. Commit 770a2aec.
- Both ride the `siege` suite (kludge-mode tadmor — NO depot/GAR/OAuth needed,
  so this does not touch the depot another chat may be working).

GOAL: prove `siege` runs green on BOTH rocket Docker substrates —
  - `ssh wsl@rocket`    (WSL Ubuntu, Docker daemon live)
  - `ssh cygwin@rocket` (Cygwin + Windows Docker Desktop)
The repo is cloned at ~/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster on each account.

TRANSPORT DISCIPLINE (git is the filesystem — see the ₣BV paddock):
1. On the curia (this station): ensure main is reconciled and pushed
   (`git pull --rebase` then `git push`); the local branch is currently ahead
   of origin.
2. On each fundus over ssh: fast-forward to origin
   (`git fetch && git checkout -f main && git reset --hard origin/main`).
3. Run `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.siege.sh` over ssh (no trailing echo, no pipes —
   let ssh propagate the exit code; siege refuses a dirty tree). Do NOT scp.

WHAT TO WATCH:
- `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_pipeline_selfcheck` must PASS everywhere (pure logic).
- `rbtdrc_sortie_offpath_blocked_dest` should PASS (NoResponse→SECURE) on a
  healthy substrate. On Windows Docker Desktop (cygwin), if it BREACHes — a
  reply of ANY provenance came back to a blocked destination — that is a REAL
  FINDING (it voids the rbsq_wdd_offpath_reply quirk's benign classification),
  not a flake. Capture the full verdict and stop to characterize it.
- `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` should stay SECURE.

ON SUCCESS: report both legs' results. If both green, the ₢BVAAJ docket's
"Done" (WSL + Cygwin rocket legs) is met — ask the operator before wrapping.
If cygwin surfaces a breach, write it up as a finding and do not wrap.

NOTE: Payor OAuth on rocket may be stale, but siege is kludge-mode and should
not need it. If any case demands GCP credentials, stop — that means something
is mis-scoped; report rather than refreshing depot state.
```

---

One honesty flag on the prompt: I've written it for the **direct-ssh** path (the ad-hoc `wsl@rocket` / `cygwin@rocket` accounts from CLAUDE.md), not the JJK Foray/legatio protocol — simpler for a one-shot suite run. If you'd rather that chat use `jjx_relay` foray dispatch, tell me and I'll rewrite it that way.
